clinicalStem: A 68-year-old woman underwent uncomplicated phacoemulsification with posterior chamber IOL implantation 14 months ago. Her vision had improved to 6/6 post-operatively, but she now presents with a 3-month history of gradually worsening blurred vision, glare, and difficulty reading. Slit-lamp examination reveals a wrinkled, fibrotic posterior capsule behind the IOL. The rest of the anterior segment is unremarkable and IOP is 14 mmHg. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Reassure the patient and schedule a 6-month review
Nd:YAG laser posterior capsulotomy
Surgical IOL explantation and capsulectomy
Prescribe topical corticosteroid drops for 4 weeks
Repeat phacoemulsification in the affected eye
explanation: Posterior capsule opacification (PCO) is the most common late complication of cataract surgery, caused by residual lens epithelial cell proliferation and migration across the posterior capsule. The definitive and minimally invasive treatment is Nd:YAG laser posterior capsulotomy, which creates a clear central opening in the opacified capsule, restoring vision immediately. Surgical intervention is unnecessary when laser treatment is available and safe.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Reassurance and watchful waiting are inappropriate as the patient has symptomatic, progressive vision loss from a treatable cause.
Nd:YAG laser posterior capsulotomy is the correct, first-line, minimally invasive definitive treatment for PCO.
Surgical IOL explantation is reserved for rare cases of lens dislocation or severe IOL-related complications, not for PCO.
Topical corticosteroids treat inflammation, not fibrotic capsular opacification; they have no role here.
Repeat phacoemulsification is not indicated; the PCO is a posterior capsule issue, not a new cataract.
clinicalPearl: PCO is the most common late complication after cataract surgery; Nd:YAG laser capsulotomy is safe, quick, and restores vision with minimal risk.
keyClue: Vision improved post-operatively then gradually worsened over months with a fibrotic posterior capsule seen on slit-lamp.
commonExamTrap: Mistaking PCO for a new cataract or intraocular inflammation and prescribing steroids instead of arranging laser treatment.
missedConcept: PCO is managed with Nd:YAG laser — not surgery, not drops; the IOL remains in place.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old hyperopic woman presents to the emergency department at 11 PM with a 4-hour history of severe right-sided headache, nausea, vomiting, and markedly blurred vision with coloured haloes around lights. On examination the right eye is red, the cornea is hazy and oedematous, the pupil is mid-dilated and fixed, and the eye feels hard on palpation. IOP is measured at 58 mmHg. Which of the following is the most important IMMEDIATE pharmacological measure?
options: Topical pilocarpine 4% applied to the right eye only
Intravenous acetazolamide 500 mg plus topical timolol and apraclonidine
Oral glycerol 1 g/kg
Topical dexamethasone drops four times daily
Arrange urgent Nd:YAG laser iridotomy within 30 minutes
explanation: Acute angle-closure glaucoma with IOP of 58 mmHg is an ocular emergency threatening permanent visual loss within hours. Immediate multi-agent pharmacological IOP reduction is the priority: IV acetazolamide rapidly reduces aqueous production; topical beta-blockers (timolol) and alpha-agonists (apraclonidine) synergistically lower IOP. This combination achieves the fastest and most substantial acute IOP reduction to protect the optic nerve while the eye is prepared for definitive laser or surgical peripheral iridotomy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pilocarpine alone is insufficient for an IOP of 58 mmHg and the ischaemic, dilated iris sphincter may not respond adequately; it is used as adjunct after IOP begins to fall.
IV acetazolamide combined with topical timolol and apraclonidine is the correct immediate multi-drug IOP-lowering regimen.
Oral glycerol is a hyperosmotic agent used as adjunct; it is not the first-line acute intervention and is contraindicated in diabetics.
Topical steroids address corneal oedema and inflammation but do not lower IOP and are not the priority at presentation.
Laser iridotomy is the definitive treatment but cannot be performed safely on a hazy oedematous cornea until IOP is first pharmacologically reduced.
clinicalPearl: In acute angle-closure glaucoma, lower IOP immediately with IV acetazolamide + topical timolol + apraclonidine before attempting laser iridotomy — the cornea must clear first.
keyClue: IOP 58 mmHg, mid-dilated fixed pupil, hazy cornea, coloured haloes — the classic triad of acute angle closure.
commonExamTrap: Choosing laser iridotomy as the first action — this is correct definitively but is not possible on a hazy cornea and IOP must be reduced first.
missedConcept: Definitive treatment (iridotomy) requires prior pharmacological IOP reduction; skipping this step risks optic nerve damage during a potentially prolonged wait.
redFlagReason: Acute angle-closure glaucoma with IOP 58 mmHg is a true ocular emergency — irreversible optic nerve and retinal damage occurs within hours if IOP is not reduced urgently.

clinicalStem: A 71-year-old man with a 5-year history of primary open-angle glaucoma is reviewed. He has been on latanoprost 0.005% once nightly since diagnosis. His target IOP was set at 18 mmHg. Today his IOP is 17 mmHg in both eyes. However, repeat Humphrey visual field testing shows a new superior nasal step in the right eye compared with the test done 18 months ago, and his optic disc photographs show enlargement of the cup-to-disc ratio from 0.65 to 0.75 in the same eye. What is the most appropriate next management step?
options: Continue current treatment and repeat fields in 12 months
Add topical brimonidine to the right eye and reassess in 3 months
Lower the target IOP and intensify treatment — consider adding a prostaglandin analogue or referring for surgery/laser
Switch from latanoprost to timolol as the primary agent
Reassure the patient as IOP is within the target range
explanation: Structural (cup enlargement) and functional (new visual field defect) progression despite IOP within the stated target range indicates that the current target IOP is insufficient for this patient. Management requires reassessing and lowering the target IOP further and escalating therapy — this may include adding a second agent, laser trabeculoplasty, or surgical referral (trabeculectomy). IOP-lowering remains the only proven intervention to slow glaucomatous progression.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Continuing unchanged treatment ignores objective evidence of structural and functional progression — this is incorrect and potentially harmful.
Adding brimonidine to one eye alone is a partial measure; the correct step is to formally lower the target IOP and systematically escalate bilateral treatment.
Lowering the target IOP and escalating treatment is the correct response to documented progression despite current therapy.
Simply switching agents without lowering the target IOP does not address the core problem of progressive glaucomatous damage.
Reassurance is dangerous when there is objective evidence of optic nerve progression and new visual field loss.
clinicalPearl: Glaucoma progression despite 'on-target' IOP means the target was set too high — lower it and escalate treatment rather than accepting the current regimen.
keyClue: New visual field defect AND cup-to-disc ratio enlargement despite IOP within target range — the target IOP is inadequate.
commonExamTrap: Assuming that an IOP at or below target means treatment is adequate — structural/functional progression overrides numerical targets.
missedConcept: Target IOP must be individualized and revised downward if progression occurs, regardless of where IOP sits relative to the original target.

clinicalStem: A 48-year-old man with a 22-year history of type 1 diabetes mellitus and poorly controlled HbA1c (9.8%) presents with sudden, painless loss of vision in the left eye on waking. He describes seeing floaters and a dark red haze. Visual acuity is hand movements in the left eye. Fundoscopy reveals a dense vitreous haemorrhage obscuring the posterior pole; the right eye shows pre-retinal neovascularisation. B-scan ultrasound of the left eye shows no retinal detachment. What is the most appropriate management of the left eye at this stage?
options: Arrange urgent vitrectomy within 24 hours
Admit for strict bed rest with bilateral eye padding for 7 days
Commence intravitreal anti-VEGF (e.g., ranibizumab) immediately and reassess in 4 weeks
Observe with weekly review for spontaneous clearance; if no clearance in 1–3 months, arrange vitrectomy
Apply pan-retinal photocoagulation to the left eye immediately
explanation: A first-episode vitreous haemorrhage secondary to proliferative diabetic retinopathy in the absence of retinal detachment is typically managed conservatively initially, as many haemorrhages clear spontaneously within weeks to months. The patient is reviewed frequently (every 1–4 weeks); if haemorrhage fails to clear within approximately 1–3 months, or if a tractional retinal detachment develops, pars plana vitrectomy is indicated. Anti-VEGF may be used adjunctively but is not the primary definitive step.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Urgent vitrectomy within 24 hours is reserved for vitreous haemorrhage with concurrent tractional retinal detachment or in a monocular patient — not for a first-episode bleed with no detachment.
Bed rest and eye padding have no proven benefit in speeding clearance of vitreous haemorrhage and are not standard management.
Intravitreal anti-VEGF can reduce neovascularisation and may help, but it is not the primary initial management for vitreous haemorrhage without detachment; conservative observation is first.
Conservative observation with regular review and vitrectomy if no clearance in 1–3 months is the standard evidence-based approach for non-clearing vitreous haemorrhage without retinal detachment.
PRP cannot be safely applied through a dense vitreous haemorrhage; it is performed after clearance or at the time of vitrectomy.
clinicalPearl: A first vitreous haemorrhage from PDR without retinal detachment is observed for spontaneous clearance (1–3 months); vitrectomy is reserved for non-clearing haemorrhage or concurrent tractional detachment.
keyClue: B-scan confirms no retinal detachment — this changes the urgency from surgical emergency to monitored observation.
commonExamTrap: Rushing to immediate vitrectomy without confirming retinal detachment status; also attempting PRP through an opaque vitreous.
missedConcept: B-scan ultrasound is essential to rule out tractional retinal detachment before deciding between urgent surgery and watchful waiting.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old woman with high myopia (−9.00 D) presents with a 2-day history of new flashing lights and a 'curtain' descending from above in her right eye. She reports no distortion of central vision and can read small print. OCT and indirect ophthalmoscopy confirm a superior rhegmatogenous retinal detachment that has not yet reached the macula. What is the most important prognostic determinant for her final visual outcome, and what does it indicate about timing of surgery?
options: The axial length of the eye — longer eyes have universally poor outcomes regardless of macula involvement
The size of the retinal break — breaks larger than 3 disc diameters always result in macular detachment
Macula-on status — the macula is still attached, making urgent repair within 24 hours critical to preserve central vision
Duration of symptoms — symptoms for more than 48 hours predict irreversible photoreceptor loss in all cases
The presence of lattice degeneration — this is the primary determinant of visual recovery
explanation: The single most important prognostic factor for final visual acuity in rhegmatogenous retinal detachment is whether the macula is attached or detached at the time of surgery. A macula-on detachment, where central vision is preserved, carries an excellent visual prognosis if repaired urgently (ideally within 24 hours) before the detachment extends to involve the macula. Any delay risks macula-off conversion, which dramatically worsens long-term central visual recovery.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Axial length (high myopia) is a risk factor for retinal detachment but does not independently determine visual outcome — macula status does.
Break size influences surgical difficulty but is not the primary determinant of final visual acuity; macula status is paramount.
Macula-on status with urgent repair is correct — this is the key prognostic determinant and the reason for emergency theatre prioritisation.
Duration of 48 hours does not uniformly predict irreversible loss; the critical variable is whether the detachment has reached the fovea, not time alone.
Lattice degeneration is a predisposing factor for retinal detachment but is not a determinant of post-operative visual recovery.
clinicalPearl: Macula-on retinal detachment is a surgical emergency — operate within 24 hours to preserve central vision; once the macula detaches, the prognosis for reading vision deteriorates significantly.
keyClue: Central vision preserved, macula still attached on examination — this is the critical finding mandating urgent surgery.
commonExamTrap: Treating a macula-on detachment as semi-elective because the patient can still see well — the urgency is precisely because central vision has not yet been lost.
missedConcept: Macula-on versus macula-off status is the single most important prognostic factor in retinal detachment surgery.
redFlagReason: A macula-on retinal detachment is a time-critical ocular emergency — delay beyond 24 hours risks macular involvement and permanent central visual loss.

clinicalStem: A 74-year-old woman with known dry AMD in both eyes presents with a 5-day history of sudden metamorphopsia and reduced central vision in her right eye. Her BCVA has fallen from 6/9 to 6/36 since her last visit 3 months ago. OCT of the right eye shows a subretinal hyperreflective lesion with adjacent subretinal fluid and intraretinal fluid. Fluorescein angiography demonstrates a classic subfoveal choroidal neovascular membrane. Which of the following best describes the most appropriate treatment and the expected natural history without treatment?
options: Thermal laser photocoagulation to the subfoveal CNV; without treatment, 90% of patients retain 6/12 vision at 2 years
Intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy (e.g., ranibizumab or aflibercept); without treatment, most patients with subfoveal classic CNV will lose 3 or more lines of vision within 3 months
Photodynamic therapy with verteporfin as first-line; natural history is benign with spontaneous regression in 60% of cases
Oral antioxidant supplements (AREDS formula); without treatment, subfoveal CNV rarely progresses to legal blindness
Subretinal surgery to excise the CNV membrane; anti-VEGF is reserved for extrafoveal lesions only
explanation: Intravitreal anti-VEGF therapy (ranibizumab, aflibercept, bevacizumab) is the first-line treatment for neovascular (wet) AMD with subfoveal classic CNV. Without treatment, classic subfoveal CNV carries a very poor natural history — the majority of untreated eyes lose three or more lines of visual acuity within three months due to progressive subretinal haemorrhage, exudate, and disciform scarring. Anti-VEGF has transformed outcomes, achieving stabilisation or improvement in most treated eyes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Thermal laser is contraindicated for subfoveal CNV because it destroys the overlying foveal photoreceptors; it is only used for extrafoveal lesions.
Intravitreal anti-VEGF with its stated natural history is the correct answer.
PDT with verteporfin was a pre-anti-VEGF treatment for classic subfoveal CNV but is no longer first-line; spontaneous regression in 60% is incorrect.
Oral AREDS supplements reduce progression from intermediate to advanced dry AMD — they have no role in treating established wet AMD with active CNV.
Subretinal surgery for CNV is rarely performed and outcomes are poor; anti-VEGF is first-line for all subfoveal lesions regardless of position.
clinicalPearl: Untreated classic subfoveal CNV in wet AMD rapidly destroys central vision within weeks to months — prompt anti-VEGF injection is vision-saving.
keyClue: Classic subfoveal CNV on FFA with subretinal and intraretinal fluid on OCT — wet AMD conversion requiring immediate anti-VEGF.
commonExamTrap: Recommending thermal laser for subfoveal CNV — this destroys the fovea and is absolutely contraindicated subfoveally.
missedConcept: Anti-VEGF has replaced PDT and laser as standard of care for neovascular AMD; early treatment is essential given the aggressive natural history.

topic: Dry eye disease — complications of untreated OSD
clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman with long-standing dry eye syndrome (keratoconjunctivitis sicca) associated with primary Sjögren syndrome presents for review. She has been inconsistently using artificial tears and has not been seen for 18 months. She now complains of worsening pain, photophobia, and decreased vision. Slit-lamp examination with fluorescein staining reveals confluent epithelial staining in the inferior third and central cornea, with several dense punctate erosions coalescing into a band of superficial keratitis. TBUT is 3 seconds. Which of the following complications is this patient at highest immediate risk for if left untreated?
options: Cataract formation from chronic anterior chamber inflammation
Bacterial corneal ulcer (infectious keratitis) secondary to epithelial barrier breakdown
Angle-closure glaucoma from aqueous insufficiency
Optic neuritis from lacrimal gland inflammation extending posteriorly
Proliferative vitreoretinopathy from chronic ocular surface disease
explanation: Severe, untreated dry eye disease leads to progressive corneal epithelial breakdown. When the epithelial barrier is disrupted, as evidenced by confluent fluorescein staining and epithelial erosions, the cornea loses its primary defense against microbial invasion. The most immediate and serious sight-threatening complication is bacterial (infectious) keratitis, which can rapidly progress to corneal perforation and permanent vision loss, particularly in Sjögren-associated severe dry eye.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Cataract from anterior chamber inflammation is not a direct consequence of ocular surface disease; the anterior chamber is not primarily involved in dry eye.
Bacterial corneal ulcer from epithelial barrier breakdown is the correct and most immediate serious complication.
Angle-closure glaucoma is caused by anatomical angle narrowing, not aqueous tear deficiency; these are entirely separate pathological processes.
Optic neuritis does not result from lacrimal gland disease and is not a complication of dry eye syndrome.
Proliferative vitreoretinopathy is a complication of retinal detachment surgery, not ocular surface disease.
clinicalPearl: Severe OSD with epithelial breakdown dramatically increases the risk of sight-threatening infectious keratitis — confluent corneal staining demands urgent, aggressive management.
keyClue: Confluent epithelial staining with coalescing erosions in Sjögren-associated severe dry eye — the epithelial barrier is failing.
commonExamTrap: Underestimating the severity of dry eye and treating it purely as a comfort issue rather than recognising the risk of infectious keratitis.
missedConcept: Corneal epithelial integrity is the primary defense against infection; severe dry eye that destroys this barrier creates a portal for bacterial keratitis.

clinicalStem: A 9-year-old boy presents with a 3-day history of right-sided periorbital swelling, fever (38.9°C), and reduced eye movements. On examination there is proptosis, chemosis, and painful restriction of all ocular movements in the right eye. Vision is 6/9 right and 6/6 left. CRP is 145 mg/L. CT orbit with contrast shows a right subperiosteal abscess along the medial orbital wall with adjacent ethmoid sinusitis. He is started on IV broad-spectrum antibiotics. After 36 hours he develops new severe headache, vomiting, neck stiffness, and his vision in the right eye falls to counting fingers. Which complication has most likely occurred and what is the most urgent next step?
options: Worsening orbital cellulitis — increase antibiotic doses and add antifungal cover
Cavernous sinus thrombosis or intracranial extension — immediate neurosurgical and ENT consultation with urgent MRI brain/orbits and reassess surgical drainage
Orbital apex syndrome — this is an expected progression; continue current antibiotics for a further 5 days
Septic emboli to the retina — arrange urgent fluorescein angiography
Antibiotic-related optic neuritis — stop current antibiotics and switch to an alternative regimen
explanation: New neurological symptoms (severe headache, vomiting, neck stiffness) with rapid visual deterioration despite IV antibiotics in orbital cellulitis indicates intracranial extension, most dangerously cavernous sinus thrombosis or intracranial abscess. This is a neurosurgical emergency: urgent MRI brain and orbits is needed to define the extent, and immediate multidisciplinary (neurosurgery + ENT + ophthalmology) intervention — including surgical drainage — is required. Failure to act urgently risks permanent visual loss, stroke, and death.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Simply increasing antibiotics without neurosurgical involvement is dangerously inadequate for suspected intracranial extension with meningism.
Cavernous sinus thrombosis/intracranial extension with urgent MRI and multidisciplinary surgical consultation is the correct response.
Orbital apex syndrome is a recognised complication but does not explain meningism; treating it as expected and continuing antibiotics alone is incorrect.
Septic emboli causing sudden visual loss are possible but do not explain meningism; FFA would not address the intracranial emergency.
Antibiotic-related optic neuritis is extremely rare and does not explain fever, meningism, and rapid bilateral neural deterioration in this context.
clinicalPearl: New headache, neck stiffness, or neurological decline in orbital cellulitis signals intracranial extension (cavernous sinus thrombosis or abscess) — this is a neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate MRI and multidisciplinary intervention.
keyClue: Meningism (neck stiffness, vomiting, severe headache) combined with rapidly deteriorating vision despite IV antibiotics in orbital cellulitis.
commonExamTrap: Attributing neurological signs to worsening orbital disease alone and failing to recognise cavernous sinus thrombosis or intracranial spread.
missedConcept: Orbital cellulitis can extend intracranially — meningism mandates urgent MRI and neurosurgical consultation, not just antibiotic escalation.
redFlagReason: Cavernous sinus thrombosis and intracranial abscess complicating orbital cellulitis carry significant mortality and risk of permanent blindness — immediate neurosurgical emergency management is required.

topic: Uveitis — complications of chronic anterior uveitis
clinicalStem: A 14-year-old girl with juvenile idiopathic arthritis (oligoarticular type, ANA-positive) has been under ophthalmology surveillance for 4 years. She has had three episodes of anterior uveitis, all treated with topical steroids. She now presents with gradual visual deterioration. On examination, BCVA is 6/24 in the right eye. Slit-lamp shows a white, calcific band across the interpalpebral cornea at the level of Bowman's layer, and posterior subcapsular lens opacity. IOP is 22 mmHg. Which long-term complication is MOST responsible for her reduced vision and what is the preferred treatment for the corneal finding?
options: Cystoid macular oedema is the cause; treat with intravitreal triamcinolone
Band keratopathy causing visual axis opacification; treatment is EDTA chelation (disodium EDTA) with superficial keratectomy
Posterior synechiae causing iris bombe; treat with urgent surgical iridectomy
Steroid-induced glaucoma; treat with topical IOP-lowering agents and discontinue steroids
Corneal ulceration from immunosuppression; treat with intensive topical antibiotics
explanation: Band keratopathy — calcium deposition in Bowman's layer across the interpalpebral corneal zone — is a classic long-term complication of chronic anterior uveitis, particularly in JIA-associated uveitis. When it encroaches on the visual axis, it causes significant vision loss. The treatment is EDTA chelation: topical disodium EDTA dissolves the calcium deposits, often combined with superficial keratectomy. The posterior subcapsular cataract is an additional complication (from chronic inflammation and steroid use) but the corneal band is the most visually significant finding here.
wrongAnswerExplanations: CMO is an important uveitis complication but requires OCT to diagnose and intravitreal injection; it does not explain the visible corneal band.
Band keratopathy causing visual axis opacification treated with EDTA chelation is the correct answer for the described slit-lamp finding.
Posterior synechiae causing iris bombé would present with a shallow anterior chamber and very high IOP with pupil distortion — not described here.
Steroid-induced glaucoma is present (IOP 22 mmHg) and needs management, but it does not explain the visible band-shaped corneal opacity causing vision loss.
Corneal ulceration has a different clinical appearance (epithelial defect, infiltrate) and is not consistent with the calcific interpalpebral band described.
clinicalPearl: Band keratopathy (calcium in Bowman's layer) is a hallmark complication of JIA-associated chronic anterior uveitis and is treated with EDTA chelation when it threatens the visual axis.
keyClue: ANA-positive JIA with recurrent anterior uveitis + white calcific interpalpebral band at Bowman's layer = band keratopathy.
commonExamTrap: Attributing vision loss solely to the posterior subcapsular cataract and missing the visually significant band keratopathy requiring EDTA chelation.
missedConcept: JIA uveitis is often asymptomatic and causes insidious complications — band keratopathy, cataract, and glaucoma — requiring regular screening regardless of symptoms.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 4-day history of painful loss of vision in her right eye, worse with eye movement. VA in the right eye is 6/60; the left eye is 6/6. There is a right relative afferent pupillary defect. The optic disc appears normal on fundoscopy. MRI brain shows multiple T2/FLAIR periventricular white matter lesions. She is commenced on IV methylprednisolone. Which of the following statements BEST describes her visual prognosis and the long-term implication of her MRI findings?
options: Visual recovery to 6/6 is unlikely; the periventricular lesions indicate permanent optic nerve damage

Most patients recover visual acuity to 6/9 or better within 4–6 weeks regardless of steroid treatment; however, her MRI findings confer a significantly elevated risk (approximately 50–70%) of developing clinically definite multiple sclerosis within 10 years
IV methylprednisolone will prevent any future episodes of optic neuritis and the MRI lesions are not clinically significant
Her visual loss will be permanent unless treated with plasmapheresis within 48 hours
The normal optic disc rules out optic neuritis; an alternative diagnosis such as functional visual loss or macular disease should be sought
explanation: Optic neuritis typically carries a good visual prognosis — the Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial (ONTT) demonstrated that the majority of patients recover visual acuity to 6/9 or better within 4–6 weeks. However, the presence of two or more periventricular white matter lesions on MRI confers a dramatically elevated 10-year risk of developing clinically definite multiple sclerosis (approximately 50–72% in the ONTT). IV steroids speed recovery but do not improve final visual outcome and do not prevent MS conversion.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Visual recovery to 6/6 or near-normal is actually expected in most cases — periventricular lesions do not imply permanent optic nerve damage at this stage.
Most patients recover well and the elevated MS risk from periventricular lesions is the correct dual message from ONTT data.
IV steroids speed visual recovery but do NOT prevent future episodes of optic neuritis and the MRI lesions are highly significant, predicting MS risk.
Plasmapheresis is used for severe, steroid-refractory demyelinating disease — it is not standard first-line for optic neuritis and visual prognosis is generally good without it.
Retrobulbar optic neuritis (normal disc) is the classic presentation; 'no disc swelling, no optic neuritis' is a common and dangerous exam trap.
clinicalPearl: Optic neuritis has a good visual prognosis (most recover to 6/9+), but multiple periventricular MRI lesions signal a 50–72% 10-year MS conversion risk — IV steroids speed recovery but do not alter final vision or MS risk.
keyClue: Retrobulbar optic neuritis (normal disc) + multiple periventricular lesions on MRI = elevated MS conversion risk.
commonExamTrap: A normal optic disc leading to dismissal of optic neuritis — retrobulbar neuritis classically shows a normal disc acutely.
missedConcept: Visual prognosis in optic neuritis is generally good; the most important prognostic implication of the MRI findings is the risk of MS conversion, not permanent blindness.

topic: Thyroid eye disease — compressive optic neuropathy
clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman with known Graves' disease and active thyroid eye disease (clinical activity score 5/7) presents with a 2-week history of gradual deterioration in colour vision and contrast sensitivity bilaterally, worse on the right. Visual acuity is 6/12 right and 6/9 left. There is bilateral proptosis (right 24 mm, left 22 mm by Hertel), and visual field testing shows bilateral central scotomata. Colour vision is significantly impaired on Ishihara testing (right 4/17, left 9/17). CT orbit confirms grossly enlarged medial and inferior recti bilaterally compressing the orbital apex. What is the most urgent management priority?
options: High-dose oral selenium supplementation and review in 6 weeks
Topical lubricants and protective eyewear to prevent corneal exposure
High-dose intravenous corticosteroids (e.g., IV methylprednisolone 1 g IV) with urgent orbital decompression surgery if no response within 2–4 weeks — or sooner if deteriorating
Radioactive iodine therapy to achieve euthyroidism as the primary treatment for optic neuropathy
Lateral orbital decompression as first-line without a trial of steroids
explanation: Dysthyroid optic neuropathy (DON) is the most serious complication of thyroid eye disease and an urgent sight-threatening emergency. It is caused by compression of the optic nerve at the orbital apex by swollen extraocular muscles. Clinical signs include reduced visual acuity, colour vision loss, central scotoma, and RAPD. Initial management is high-dose IV corticosteroids (IV methylprednisolone 1 g pulse). If vision does not improve within 2–4 weeks — or deteriorates at any point — urgent orbital decompression surgery is performed to relieve apical compression.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Selenium supplementation is used in mild, inactive TED to reduce progression; it has no role in managing acute compressive optic neuropathy, which is an emergency.
Topical lubricants address corneal exposure, not compressive optic neuropathy — they are completely inadequate for this life-altering visual emergency.
IV methylprednisolone with surgical backup for non-responders is the correct evidence-based emergency management pathway for DON.
Radioactive iodine treats the thyroid, not the orbital inflammation; it can temporarily worsen TED and has no role in acute DON management.

Orbital decompression may be first-line in rapidly deteriorating or very severe DON, but most guidelines recommend a brief steroid trial first unless the patient is imminently at risk of total vision loss.
clinicalPearl: Dysthyroid optic neuropathy (colour vision loss + central scotoma + apical muscle compression on CT) is an ocular emergency — IV methylprednisolone immediately, with orbital decompression if no response.
keyClue: Bilateral central scotoma + severe colour vision loss + CT showing apical muscle compression in active Graves' disease = dysthyroid optic neuropathy.
commonExamTrap: Treating TED as a purely cosmetic or exposure problem and missing the compressive optic neuropathy indicated by colour vision and visual field loss.
missedConcept: Colour vision testing is the most sensitive clinical indicator of early optic nerve compression in thyroid eye disease.
redFlagReason: Dysthyroid optic neuropathy is a sight-threatening emergency — untreated apical compression causes permanent optic atrophy and blindness within days to weeks.

topic: Central retinal artery occlusion — prognosis and window of treatment
clinicalStem: A 67-year-old man with hypertension, atrial fibrillation (not on anticoagulation), and a 40 pack-year smoking history presents to the emergency department with sudden, painless, complete loss of vision in his left eye occurring 4 hours ago. He denies any preceding transient visual loss (amaurosis fugax). On examination, VA in the left eye is hand movements. There is a left RAPD. Fundoscopy reveals a cherry red spot at the fovea with diffuse retinal whitening and pallor. IOP is 16 mmHg bilaterally. Which of the following best describes the prognosis and the most important subsequent systemic investigation?
options: Full visual recovery is expected within 48–72 hours; no systemic workup is required as CRAO is a local ocular problem

Prognosis for significant visual recovery is very poor as retinal ischaemia >90–100 minutes is generally irreversible; urgent systemic investigation for cardioembolism (echocardiography, cardiac monitoring, carotid Doppler) is mandatory as CRAO carries a high risk of subsequent stroke and death
Central visual recovery is likely as the cherry red spot indicates foveal sparing; the main concern is glaucoma progression from the CRAO
Visual recovery is expected if urgent ocular massage and AC paracentesis are performed within 24 hours; systemic causes are not relevant in hypertensive patients
CRAO always has a benign course with spontaneous recovery; anticoagulation is the only systemic intervention required
explanation: Central retinal artery occlusion carries a poor visual prognosis because retinal neurons tolerate ischaemia for only 90–100 minutes before irreversible damage occurs; by 4 hours, significant permanent visual loss is expected. The cherry red spot is the classic fundoscopic finding reflecting preserved choroidal circulation at the fovea against an ischaemic pale retina — not foveal sparing. More critically, CRAO is a neurological emergency equivalent to a TIA or stroke: the underlying cause is almost always embolic (cardiogenic or carotid) or thrombotic, placing the patient at very high short-term risk of cerebrovascular stroke. Urgent systemic workup — echocardiography, Holter monitoring, carotid Doppler, and vascular neurology referral — is mandatory.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Full visual recovery is extremely unlikely at 4 hours; and CRAO demands urgent systemic vascular and cardiac investigation due to its strong association with impending stroke.
Poor prognosis for vision + urgent systemic cardiembolic investigation is the correct dual message for CRAO management.
The cherry red spot represents contrast between pale ischaemic retina and intact choroidal circulation at the fovea — it does not indicate foveal sparing or a favourable prognosis.

Ocular massage and AC paracentesis may be attempted within the first 2 hours to dislodge an embolus but are not effective at 4 hours; systemic causes are essential to identify regardless of aetiology.
CRAO does not have a benign course — most patients sustain permanent severe visual loss and the 90-day stroke risk following CRAO is 10–15%.
clinicalPearl: CRAO is a stroke equivalent — treat it as a neuro-vascular emergency: the visual prognosis beyond 90 minutes is poor, but urgent cardiac and carotid workup can prevent a devastating cerebral stroke.
keyClue: Cherry red spot + diffuse retinal pallor + AF not anticoagulated = central retinal artery occlusion with cardioembolism as the likely cause.
commonExamTrap: Interpreting the cherry red spot as foveal sparing (therefore good prognosis) — in CRAO it is a contrast effect, not genuine foveal preservation.
missedConcept: CRAO is a manifestation of systemic embolic disease; ignoring the systemic workup is the most dangerous management error as it misses the risk of imminent stroke.
redFlagReason: CRAO is a stroke equivalent with a 10–15% 90-day risk of cerebral stroke — urgent systemic vascular investigation is mandatory alongside ophthalmic management.
